hook.text: Google bought Android for $50 million in 2005. Today it runs on 3 billion devices — the most-used operating system ever.

buildup.text: Andy Rubin originally designed Android for digital cameras. When Google acquired it, the focus shifted to smartphones.

discovery.text: Google's product strategy was the opposite of Apple: make Android free and open-source so every phone maker would adopt it.

twist.text: The real product wasn't the OS — it was Google Search on every phone. Android was a distribution strategy for Google's services.

climax.text: By giving away the OS, Google ensured its ads reached billions. The $50 million acquisition generated trillions in search revenue.

punchline.text: Give away the razor. Sell the blades. At planetary scale.

quiz.question: What was Android's real product strategy for Google?
options: A free OS to distribute Google Search and ads to billions of phones
Competing with Apple on hardware quality
Selling the operating system to phone manufacturers
